task_completion.description: The agent used measurement tools to measure the anterior and posterior heights of the L1 vertebra on the CT scan.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The 3D Slicer interface (or similar medical imaging viewer) is visible, showing a sagittal view of the spine CT with at least two line/ruler markups placed on the anterior and posterior aspects of a vertebral body.

task_completion.description: The agent created the required JSON file in the specified directory.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A terminal, file explorer, or text editor shows a file named 'l1_compression_measurements.json' being saved or edited in the '~/Documents/SlicerData/Exports/' directory.

task_completion.description: The JSON file contains all the required fields.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The contents of the JSON file are visible and include the keys: 'vertebral_level', 'anterior_height_mm', 'posterior_height_mm', 'compression_ratio', and 'classification'.

task_completion.description: The anterior height is recorded accurately based on the synthetic CT dimensions.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The JSON file shows the 'anterior_height_mm' value is approximately 28.5 (accept values between 27.0 and 30.0 due to manual measurement variance).

task_completion.description: The posterior height is recorded accurately based on the synthetic CT dimensions.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The JSON file shows the 'posterior_height_mm' value is approximately 32.0 (accept values between 30.5 and 33.5 due to manual measurement variance).

task_completion.description: The compression ratio is correctly calculated as anterior height divided by posterior height.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The JSON file shows the 'compression_ratio' is mathematically correct based on the agent's own recorded anterior and posterior heights (expected to be around 0.89).

task_completion.description: The severity classification is correctly identified based on the compression ratio.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The JSON file shows the 'classification' is recorded as 'Normal' (or 'Grade 0'), which corresponds to a compression ratio > 0.80.

integrity.description: The agent interacted with the medical imaging software GUI to perform the measurements.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the 3D Slicer application open with the CT volume loaded and active measurement markups, rather than just a terminal window or text editor.

integrity.description: The values recorded in the JSON file match the actual measurements taken in the software.
integrity.visual_evidence: The measurement lengths displayed on the rulers/lines in the 3D Slicer UI match the values typed into the JSON file, proving the agent didn't just guess or hardcode the expected answers.

integrity.description: The agent did not bypass the manual measurement task by running a script that automatically generates the perfect expected JSON.
integrity.visual_evidence: The trajectory shows manual placement of fiducials/rulers and manual or standard programmatic extraction of those specific markup values, rather than a single bash command echoing the exact expected JSON string.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The L1 vertebra in this synthetic CT scan has an expected anterior height of 28.5 mm and a posterior height of 32.0 mm. The expected compression ratio (anterior/posterior) is approximately 0.8906. According to the Genant semi-quantitative method, a height loss of ~11% (ratio of 0.89) is classified as 'Normal' (or Grade 0). Accept slight variations in the agent's measurements (e.g., +/- 1.5 mm) due to manual markup placement, as long as the ratio is calculated correctly from their own measurements and the classification logically follows.
